stakes problem smart consent agent flow workflow edge cases live shipped
case study · video kyc · surepass

some people need a human to get verified. most just need us to get out of the way.

surepass builds identity infrastructure for banks and fintechs. i designed its video kyc end to end: a live-agent flow for people who need help, and an ai "smart consent" flow that verifies everyone else in about a minute, with no agent at all.

live in production, used by real users
role product designer, research to handoff, alongside engineering surfaces mobile · web · agent · admin
live
in production, with real users verifying daily
2 paths
agent-led and fully automated, one finish line
4 surfaces
user, agent, admin and auditor views
20+ clients
~100 kycs per client a day, in the early stage
why it matters
1 in 3
people who start a kyc in india never finish it. across the industry, abandonment runs 25–40%.

kyc is usually the first thing a new customer does, and one of the most common places they quit. it's also where cost hides. how you verify someone changes the economics completely:

physical, in-branch kycstaff, paper, travel
₹200–500
video kyc, agent-ledlive officer on a call
₹50–150
automated ai kycno human in the loop
₹3–5

per-verification ranges, indian market. sources: deepvue, signzy, hyperverge.

so the prize was clear: move as many people as safely possible from the ₹50–150 agent call to the ₹3–5 automated path, without losing the ones who genuinely need a person.

the box i had to design inside

this isn't a flow you can just make friendlier. since 2020, rbi treats a properly run video kyc (v-cip) as legally equal to meeting someone in person, but only if it clears a strict checklist:

  • consent and an entity-initiated session
  • live capture of id, no uploads
  • liveness check and face match
  • randomised questions during the session
  • geo-tagging cross-checked with ip
  • encrypted recording, retained by mandate
  • maker–checker review by a second officer
  • concurrent audit before the account goes live

every one of those controls is a place a real person can get stuck. my job was to satisfy all of them and still make it feel like a minute of someone's day. (rbi v-cip guidelines)

the problem

one flow was doing two jobs. every verification, the easy ones and the hard ones, went through a live agent.

that's fine for someone who's confused. it's a tax on everyone else: queues at peak hours, throughput chained to how many agents are online, and a cost per check that never drops. two very different people were being handed the same slow door.

the self-server

confident · in a hurry

clean document, good light, no questions. wants to finish alone, in a minute, at any hour. to them, a queue is a wall.

the guided user

unsure · edge-case document

nervous, or holding something the automation trips on. a human lowers the fear and stops a hard dead-end.

i didn't want to pick a side. i wanted two doors to the same room, and a way to send each person to the right one.

the idea · smart consent

build a path with no agent, and make the trust hold anyway.

recorded video consent and ocr handled two of the three things an agent used to guarantee. the hard one was the third: is this a real, live person, here right now?

the answer is simple enough to say in one sentence: the user speaks a one-time code out loud, on camera, while their face is detected. a fresh code you say in real time is something a photo can't fake and a recording can't guess. the agent's core judgment became a step in the flow.

one small decision carried the whole thing: the code appears only after the face is aligned in the frame. show it any earlier and people read it out from anywhere, face out of frame, or someone else's voice entirely. gate it on the face, and the moment of speaking is exactly the moment the camera needs. when the user stops speaking, transcription and matching run on their own.

and because a verification you can retry forever isn't a verification, attempts are limited. the exact count is admin-configurable, and before the final try the flow says so in plain words: this is your last attempt. the flow itself flexes too, two steps or three depending on the client's config, with everything mirroring live to their admin portal.

the other door · agent-led

for everyone the automation can't clear, the human is still there. just faster.

same visual language as the self-serve path, so being routed to an agent never feels like a downgrade. the screen is built to make one person's judgment quick and consistent.

under the hood

one verification, three roles. here's the whole machine, in the time it takes to scroll it.

admin agent user automated check
admin
creates the customer, every id up front
the agent never starts from a blank form.
admin
sets the rules
precision thresholds, retry limits, 2- or 3-step config.
admin
triggers the kyc link, one click
the link opens on the user's phone
user
five gates before entry
camera · mic · location · bandwidth · vpn. any one fails, entry is blocked, with the reason said plainly. no silent mid-call failures.
user
the lobby: queue position and timer, visible
short on time? schedule a slot instead of abandoning.
a waiting customer auto-fires the call. the agent can't decline, only go offline
agent
call opens pre-filled
agent · user
captures the document and the face
the user gets a frame to hold their face, a frame to hold their card. no guessing.
agent
ocr → government portal, live
✱ wrong read? cross-check on the call, edit the id number, fetch again. a fix, not a failure.
agent → user
mirrored questions
the agent clicks, the question appears on the user's screen. yes or no.
agent
review, then drop the call
dropping triggers the automated checks.
automated
face match and liveness, two graphs side by side
⚠ the liveness trap: liveness can score 100% on the wrong person. face-match % is the real signal, so the agent judges the pair, never one number.
agent
accept · reject · drop
everything syncs back to the admin panel.
admin
re-verified against the session video
🔒 the collusion guard: an agent might know the customer, so nothing goes live until a higher authority re-checks the data against the recording.
user
feedback closes the loop
when it doesn't go smoothly

real verification is mostly edge cases. a connection drops. every agent is busy. someone taps away mid-session.

in kyc, a user who falls out of the flow is usually a lost customer. so the unhappy path got as much attention as the happy one. the rule was simple: never dead-end a user. always leave a door open.

see it running

this isn't a concept deck. it's shipped, and people use it.

both flows run in production at surepass. here's a screen recording of the real thing, and screens captured from live sessions, browser bar and all.

how the two connect

nobody picks a "kyc type" they don't understand. everyone starts on the fast path; the moment it can't be sure, a failed check, a missing agent, a dropped call, it routes to a human or a scheduled slot, no restart. the self-server never meets a queue. the guided user never hits a wall.

and the ai, in two places
in the product — face detection gates the flow, voice otp proves liveness, ocr reads the documents. together they do the checking an agent once did by eye.
in how i designed it — claude and chatgpt for flow logic, edge-case lists and microcopy; lovable for quick clickable prototypes; gemini and ux pilot for ideation and design audits. roughly a third faster to handoff, which mostly bought me more time on the judgment calls above.
decisions underneath

  1. fast path by default, human by exception

    start everyone on smart consent; bring in an agent only when the automation isn't sure.

  2. feedback, not spinners

    green and red rings turn the most failure-prone moment into a two-second self-correction.

  3. never dead-end

    drops, busy agents and reschedules all keep a door open. that's the difference between a lost step and a lost customer.

  4. compliance as a design constraint, not an afterthought

    consent, liveness, randomised questions and maker–checker were built into the flow, not bolted on.

  5. show the evidence, not a verdict

    liveness can score 100% on the wrong person. so the agent sees face-match and liveness as two graphs, side by side, and judges the pair.

  6. design as if collusion will happen

    calls auto-assign so agents can't pick their customers, and no approval goes live until admin re-verifies it against the session video. trust the people, and still verify them.

what shipped

an agent-free path that moves verifications from ₹50–150 economics toward ₹3–5. a human path that catches everyone else. zero dead ends between them.

the system runs in production at surepass across more than 20 client businesses — banks and fintechs — each running around a hundred verifications a day, and that was just the early stage. call it 2,000+ real people verified daily through screens i designed. client names stay off a public page; i'm happy to get specific in a conversation.

what surprised me, what i'd still change

i half-expected the voice otp to feel gimmicky. it didn't. being told exactly what to say turned out to be the calmest part of the flow: people don't mind instructions, they mind ambiguity.

the part i'd still fix is the consent screen. reading a block of legal text aloud on camera is a lot to ask. i sketched a teleprompter version, text that scrolls and highlights as you speak, with your details pre-filled, that i didn't get to ship. that's the first thing i'd build next.

most of this project was invisible: making sure that when something went wrong, a dropped call, a busy queue, a document the machine couldn't read, the person on the other end still had somewhere to go. that's the part i'm proudest of.
